title: Comparison of fertility rates that account for survival of children
subtitle: The [total fertility rate](#dod:fertility-rate) considers all births per woman. In contrast, the effective reproductive fertility rate considers only those expected to survive to their own childbearing years, and the effective labor fertility rate considers only those expected to survive to working age.
note: Childbearing years is defined by the authors as the average of 15–49 years, while working age is defined as the average of 15–65 years old.
caption:
Comparison of fertility rates that account for survival of children

The total fertility rate considers all births per woman. In contrast, the effective reproductive fertility rate considers only those expected to survive to their own childbearing years, and the effective labor fertility rate considers only those expected to survive to working age.

This line chart tracks three distinct fertility metrics for India from 1950 to 2023. The vertical axis measures the number of children per woman, ranging from 0 to 6, while the horizontal axis marks the years. The top line, colored teal, represents the total fertility rate. It begins near 5.8 in 1950, rises slightly to a peak of approximately 6.0 around 1965, and then follows a consistent downward trajectory, ending just above 2.0 in 2023. Below this are two closely clustered lines: a brown line representing the effective fertility rate for children surviving until childbearing years, and a purple line for those surviving until working age. These two lower lines start between 3.5 and 3.8 in 1950, rise to a peak of roughly 4.1 around 1965, and decline steadily thereafter. The gap between the total fertility rate and the two effective rates is widest during the mid-20th century, indicating a larger difference between total births and surviving children during that period. As time progresses toward 2023, all three lines converge, with the effective rates dropping just below 2.0. The note specifies that childbearing years are defined as the average of 15–49 years, while working age is defined as the average of 15–65 years old.
